text: Positive pacing (slowing down over the race) is the most common strategy observed in marathon competitions, reported in approximately 77% of studies reviewed.
Negative pacing (speeding up over the race) is relatively uncommon in marathons, reported in approximately 18% of studies reviewed.
Even pacing in marathons is rare, reported in approximately 13% of studies reviewed.
Gender and race distance are the factors with the most substantial effects on marathon pacing strategy selection.
Marathon pacing strategies are influenced by gender, age, performance level, pack running, and physiological and psychological factors.
Pacing profiles (the shape of speed across segments) and pacing ranges (the magnitude of speed variation) are the primary indicators for characterizing marathon pacing strategies.
Marathon pacing strategy is dynamic rather than fixed, changing in response to race conditions and athlete characteristics.
